name: AWS SDK for Python (Boto3)
what_it_does: The primary Python SDK for Amazon Web Services, providing a unified interface to 200+ AWS services including S3, EC2, Lambda, DynamoDB, SQS, and more. Agents use it to orchestrate cloud resources, read/write data, trigger serverless functions, and automate infrastructure.
use_cases: Reading and writing files to S3 from agent workflows
Invoking Lambda functions as sub-agents or tool calls
Querying DynamoDB for low-latency key-value lookups
Publishing messages to SQS or SNS for async task queuing
Managing EC2 instances, ECS tasks, or EKS clusters programmatically
Interacting with Bedrock for LLM inference within AWS environments
Automating IAM role creation and permission management
not_for: Non-AWS cloud providers (use GCP or Azure SDKs instead)
Environments where pip install is not available
Agents needing REST-only access without Python runtime
best_when: An agent operates within AWS or needs to orchestrate AWS services — S3, Lambda, DynamoDB, Bedrock — from Python code.
avoid_when: Your infrastructure is on GCP or Azure, or you need a thin REST API rather than a full SDK dependency.

auth.notes: Uses AWS credential chain: environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY), ~/.aws/credentials file, EC2/ECS instance profile, or IAM role via STS assume-role. For agents in AWS, IAM roles are strongly preferred over static keys. Supports STS for temporary credentials and cross-account access.

pricing.free_tier_limits: SDK itself is free; costs are incurred per AWS service used (S3 storage/requests, Lambda invocations, etc.)
pricing.paid_tiers: Pay-per-use per underlying AWS service — S3 from $0.023/GB, Lambda $0.20/million requests, DynamoDB from $1.25/million write units
pricing.notes: Boto3 is open-source and free. AWS services themselves are pay-as-you-go. AWS Free Tier covers significant usage for 12 months (e.g., 5GB S3, 1M Lambda requests/month). Cost accumulation is a real risk for agents — always set billing alerts.

performance.rate_limits: Per-service and per-API; S3 3,500 PUT/s, 5,500 GET/s per prefix; Lambda 10 concurrent by default (soft limit); DynamoDB provisioned or on-demand throughput

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Boto3 raises typed exceptions (botocore.exceptions.ClientError) with an error response dict containing Code and Message. Most errors include a RequestId for AWS support. Pagination errors can be confusing — agents often forget to handle Truncated flags or paginator setup.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: S3 PutObject is idempotent. DynamoDB PutItem and conditional writes support idempotency. Lambda invocations are not idempotent by default — use client token on async invocations. SQS has at-least-once delivery requiring deduplication IDs on FIFO queues.
known_agent_gotchas: Credential chain order matters — a stale ~/.aws/credentials file will override environment variables in some configurations
Pagination is not automatic — agents must use paginators or check NextToken/IsTruncated on list operations
Region must be explicitly set — operations against wrong region fail with EndpointResolutionError
Boto3 client vs resource distinction confuses many agents — clients are low-level, resources are higher-level OOP wrappers
Rate limit errors (ThrottlingException) require exponential backoff — not handled automatically for all services
IAM permission errors return AccessDeniedException with minimal detail about which policy is missing
S3 key naming affects performance — avoid sequential prefixes at high scale (they hash-partition by prefix)
Lambda cold starts can cause agent timeouts — set appropriate timeout values on both Lambda and calling client

security_score_components.security_notes: Full AWS IAM security model — see aws-api. Boto3 is the standard Python SDK for AWS. IAM roles preferred over long-lived credentials. Credential chain handles env vars, ~/.aws/credentials, instance profiles, ECS task roles.
